page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.n4.xlarge is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine instance powered by a shared Intel Xeon 8163 processor running at 2.5 GHz. It features 4 vCPUs, 4 physical cores with 1.0 thread per core, and 8.0 GB of system memory, yielding a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per core. The instance operates on the KVM hypervisor and does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware. Network connectivity is limited to a baseline bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate that the instance is positioned at the lower tier of compute capabilities. Single-core and multi-core stress-ng CPU tests place the server in the bottom 25%, while Geekbench single-core and multi-core scores rank in the bottom 10% and bottom 25% respectively. Memory performance is similarly constrained, with Passmark memory scores in the bottom 10% and read-only memory bandwidth tests falling in the bottom 25%. However, uncached large-block memory write bandwidth shows average performance in the middle 50%.

Due to its shared CPU allocation and lower-tier benchmark results, this instance offers a budget-friendly option for workloads that do not require sustained high-performance compute. It is best suited for lightweight applications, basic web serving, development environments, and non-critical background tasks.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.n4.xlarge is an x86_64 virtual machine instance configured with 4 vCPUs and 8.0 GB of RAM, running on a shared Intel Xeon 8163 processor at 2.5 GHz. Operating under the KVM hypervisor, this instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, and provides a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Benchmark data indicates a budget-oriented performance profile, with CPU and memory performance generally ranking in the bottom 10% to 25% of tested servers, though large-block memory write bandwidth reaches average levels. Given its shared CPU allocation and resource constraints, the ecs.n4.xlarge is qualitatively cost-effective for non-demanding workloads. It is best suited for lightweight web serving, software development testing, and basic utility tasks that do not require dedicated compute resources.
